front: Opera: called a 'shabby little shocker' by musicologist Joseph Kerman in Opera as Drama (1956); conductor Thomas Beecham said anything Kerman said about its composer 'can safely be ignored'
Opera: opens with a recurring motif of three loud, brassy major chords on B-flat, A-flat, and E
Opera: the title character believes her lover will undergo a mock execution, but Cavaradossi is really shot; she then leaps from the Castel Sant'Angelo
Opera: Puccini considered casting Enrico Caruso as Cavaradossi for the premiere but chose the older Emilio De Marchi instead — a decision he later regretted
Opera: the title character becomes jealous after noticing that the eyes in Cavaradossi's painting of Mary Magdalene are modeled on those of the Marchesa Attavanti
Opera: the title character betrays the hiding place of Angelotti, the former consul of the Roman Republic
Opera: Baron Scarpia offers to free Cavaradossi in exchange for sex with the title character, who agrees — then stabs him with his own knife
Opera: sung by the title character after Scarpia's blackmail; the singer claims she 'donated jewels to the Madonna's mantle' and 'never harmed a living soul'
Opera aria: begins with a descending phrase of E-flat, D-flat, B-flat, A-flat, moving from E-flat minor to E-flat major; often performed lying down
Opera aria: Giulio Gatti-Casazza said the longest ovation he saw at the Met occurred when Maria Jeritza sang this aria entirely lying prone
Opera aria: sung by Cavaradossi in Act III as he awaits execution; introduced by a clarinet solo with repeated C-sharp and F-sharp notes; the singer recalls 'sweet kisses and languorous caresses'
work: "E lucevan le stelle" (And the Stars Were Shining)

front: Opera aria: the singer laments 'I die in desperation / And I never before loved life so much'; he traded his last possession (a ring) to deliver a letter to the title character
work: "E lucevan le stelle" (And the Stars Were Shining)

front: Opera aria: Cavaradossi sings this while combining the features of the Marchesa Attavanti (blonde, blue-eyed) and Tosca (dark-haired) in his painting of Mary Magdalene
Opera: a church choir celebrates a general's presumed defeat at the Battle of Marengo by singing a Te Deum, while the villain exclaims 'You make me forget even God'
work: Act I – The Church / Te Deum Scene

front: Opera: at the start, a character searches for a key hidden in the feet of a statue of the Virgin Mary
work: Act I – The Church / Te Deum Scene

front: Opera: during a performance, Maria Callas's hair caught on fire and baritone Tito Gobbi leaped on her to put it out
